records.ety: [AS. dol foolish; akin to gedwelan to err, D. dol mad, dwalen to wander, err, G. toll mad, Goth. dwals foolish, stupid, cf. Gr. <?/ turbid, troubled, Skr. dhvr to cause to fall. Cf. Dolt, Dwale, Dwell, Fraud.]

defs: Slow of understanding; wanting readiness of apprehension; stupid; doltish; blockish.
quotes: She is not bred so dull but she can learn.

defs: Slow in action; sluggish; unready; awkward.
quotes: This people's heart is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing.
O, help my weak wit and sharpen my dull tongue.
Think me not So dull a devil to forget the loss Of such a matchless wife.

defs: Not keen in edge or point; lacking sharpness; blunt.
Not bright or clear to the eye; wanting in liveliness of color or luster; not vivid; obscure; dim; as, a dull fire or lamp; a dull red or yellow; a dull mirror.
Heavy; gross; cloggy; insensible; spiritless; lifeless; inert.
quotes: As turning the logs will make a dull fire burn, so changes of study a dull brain.

defs: Furnishing little delight, spirit, or variety; uninteresting; tedious; cheerless; gloomy; melancholy; depressing; as, a dull story or sermon; a dull occupation or period; hence, cloudy; overcast; as, a dull day.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Duller (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Dulling.]

defs: To deprive of sharpness of edge or point.
quotes: Borrowing dulls the edge of husbandry.

defs: To make dull, stupid, or sluggish; to stupefy, as the senses, the feelings, the perceptions, and the like.
quotes: Those [drugs] she has Will stupefy and dull the sense a while.
Use and custom have so dulled our eyes.

defs: To render dim or obscure; to sully; to tarnish.
To deprive of liveliness or activity; to render heavy; to make inert; to depress; to weary; to sadden.
quotes: Attention of mind . . . wasted or dulled through continuance.
